agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Event sourcing is a strong architectural fit for this loan application audit trail — but it is not the only viable approach, and the decision hinges on constraints the engineering team hasn't fully surfaced. The compliance requirements (full field-change history, point-in-time reconstruction, immutable audit log) are non-negotiable and perfectly aligned with event sourcing's strengths. However, event sourcing introduces significant operational complexity (event schema evolution, eventual consistency, read-model projection maintenance, replay infrastructure) that may be disproportionate if the only driver is audit compliance rather than complex domain workflows. I evaluate three approaches: (A) full event sourcing, (B) hybrid command-sourcing with an append-only audit log alongside the current state table, and (C) database-native temporal tables. Recommendation: Approach B — a hybrid where the loan application retains a current-state table for operational reads but every mutation is recorded as an immutable event in an append-only audit log, with projections for point-in-time reconstruction. This delivers 100% of the compliance requirements with ~40% of the operational complexity of full event sourcing, while leaving a clean evolution path to full ES if domain complexity grows.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Loan Application Command Service","responsibility":"Accepts all mutation requests (status changes, data updates, officer assignments). Validates business rules against current state, then emits domain events. Single writer per aggregate to protect invariants.","boundaries":"Owns write path only. Does not serve read queries. Publishes events to the Event Store. Enforces aggregate-level consistency (one loan application = one aggregate)."},{"name":"Event Store (Append-Only Audit Log)","responsibility":"Immutable, append-only persistence of all domain events. Each event captures: aggregate ID, event type, timestamp, actor (user/system), event payload (field-level changes with before/after values), causation ID, and correlation ID. Supports read-forward-from-version queries for replay.","boundaries":"Write-once, read-many. No updates, no deletes. Separate from operational database. Retention policy: indefinite (regulatory requirement). Physical deletes require compliance officer approval and are themselves audited."},{"name":"Current State Projection (Loan Applications Table)","responsibility":"Maintains the current materialized state of each loan application for operational queries. Updated by consuming events from the Event Store. This is the existing loan_applications table, now treated as a read model.","boundaries":"Derived data only — can be rebuilt from the Event Store at any time. Serves all operational read queries (dashboards, application processing UIs). Eventual consistency with the Event Store (typically sub-second)."},{"name":"Point-in-Time Reconstruction Service","responsibility":"Given a loan application ID and a target timestamp, replays events up to that point to reconstruct the exact state. Used by compliance team for audit investigations and regulatory reporting.","boundaries":"Read-only service. Stateless — computes on demand from Event Store. May cache results for frequently-requested snapshots."},{"name":"Snapshot Store","responsibility":"Periodically persists materialized snapshots of loan application state at known event versions. Accelerates point-in-time reconstruction and projection rebuilds for long-lived aggregates.","boundaries":"Optimization layer only. Can be deleted and rebuilt. Snapshots are taken every N events (e.g., every 50) or at significant lifecycle milestones (e.g., status transitions)."},{"name":"Audit Query API","responsibility":"Exposes compliance-oriented queries: change history for a field, diff between two points in time, all changes by a specific actor, all changes within a time window. Read-only.","boundaries":"Serves compliance team and regulatory reporting. Backed by the Event Store and optionally a dedicated audit read model (e.g., Elasticsearch or a columnar store for complex queries)."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Loan Application Command Service","to":"Event Store","contract":"appendEvents(aggregateId, expectedVersion, List<DomainEvent>) → Result<eventIds, ConcurrencyConflict>. Optimistic concurrency via expected version. Each DomainEvent includes: {eventId, aggregateId, eventType, version, timestamp, actorId, actorType, payload, causationId, correlationId, schemaVersion}.","coupling_type":"direct synchronous — write path requires Event Store availability"},{"from":"Event Store","to":"Current State Projection","contract":"Event subscription (pull-based polling or push via change stream). Projection consumes events in order per aggregate. Idempotent application — events carry version numbers for deduplication.","coupling_type":"asynchronous — projection lag is acceptable for operational reads"},{"from":"Point-in-Time Reconstruction Service","to":"Event Store","contract":"readEvents(aggregateId, fromVersion?, toVersion?, toTimestamp?) → ordered List<DomainEvent>. Also reads from Snapshot Store for optimization: getLatestSnapshotBefore(aggregateId, targetVersion).","coupling_type":"direct synchronous read"},{"from":"Audit Query API","to":"Event Store","contract":"queryEvents(filters: {aggregateId?, actorId?, eventType?, timeRange?, fieldName?}) → paginated List<DomainEvent>. May also query a dedicated audit read model.","coupling_type":"direct synchronous read"},{"from":"Loan Application Command Service","to":"Current State Projection","contract":"Reads current state to validate commands before emitting events. Alternative: load state by replaying events (pure ES). Hybrid allows reading from projection for performance.","coupling_type":"synchronous read — acceptable coupling since both are within the same bounded context"}],"data_flow":"1. User/system submits a command (e.g., UpdateLoanStatus, ModifyApplicantIncome). 2. Command Service loads current state (from projection or event replay). 3. Command Service validates business rules. 4. On success, Command Service creates domain event(s) with full change details and appends to Event Store with optimistic concurrency check. 5. Event Store persists immutably and acknowledges. 6. Command Service returns success to caller. 7. Asynchronously, the Current State Projection consumes the new event and updates the loan_applications table. 8. Asynchronously, the Audit Query read model indexes the event for compliance queries. 9. Periodically, the Snapshot Store captures materialized state at version checkpoints.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event Sourcing (Hybrid)","rationale":"The core compliance requirements — immutable history, point-in-time reconstruction, full change tracking — are the textbook use case for event sourcing. The hybrid approach retains a current-state table to avoid forcing all operational queries through event replay, which reduces read-path complexity significantly."},{"pattern":"CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)","rationale":"Natural companion to event sourcing. The write model (Event Store) and read models (current state projection, audit query model) are separated. This allows optimizing each for its access pattern: append-only writes, fast key-based reads for operations, and complex filtered queries for audit."},{"pattern":"Optimistic Concurrency Control","rationale":"Protects aggregate consistency without pessimistic locking. Each event carries a version; the Event Store rejects appends where the expected version doesn't match the current version. Essential for preventing lost updates in the loan application lifecycle."},{"pattern":"Snapshotting","rationale":"Loan applications may accumulate hundreds of events over their lifecycle (months to years). Snapshots prevent reconstruction from becoming prohibitively slow. Critical for both operational performance and compliance query response times."},{"pattern":"Immutable Append-Only Log","rationale":"Directly satisfies the regulatory requirement for an immutable audit trail. The Event Store's append-only nature makes it physically impossible to silently alter history — any correction requires a compensating event that is itself audited."},{"pattern":"Domain Events with Before/After Values","rationale":"Each event payload includes both old and new values for changed fields. This enables field-level diff queries without replaying the entire event stream, which is a common compliance team request."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Loan Application Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["LoanApplication — root aggregate. Identity: applicationId. Invariants: valid status transitions (e.g., cannot go from Denied to Approved without resubmission), required fields per status, data consistency rules. Entities: Applicant, Guarantor, CollateralItem. Value Objects: Money (loan amount), Address, EmploymentHistory, CreditScore, LoanTerms."]},{"name":"Audit & Compliance","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["AuditTrail — not a traditional aggregate but a queryable projection of domain events. No write operations except event consumption. Supports compliance queries, regulatory reports, and point-in-time reconstruction."]},{"name":"Identity & Access Management","classification":"generic","aggregates":["User/Actor — provides the actorId and actorType (loan officer, underwriter, system, applicant) that every event must carry. Buy or reuse existing IAM."]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Loan Application Management","downstream":"Audit & Compliance","relationship":"Published Language — Loan Application Management publishes domain events in a well-defined schema. Audit & Compliance consumes these events. The event schema is the published language. Audit & Compliance is a Conformist — it accepts the upstream's event schema as-is."},{"upstream":"Identity & Access Management","downstream":"Loan Application Management","relationship":"Conformist — Loan Application Management accepts IAM's user model as-is for actor identification. No translation needed; actorId is an opaque reference."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Hybrid event sourcing (Event Store + current-state projection) rather than pure event sourcing","gains":"Operational reads remain fast and familiar (SQL queries against a table). Existing dashboards, reports, and integrations continue working with minimal change. Lower cognitive load for developers unfamiliar with ES. Projection can be rebuilt from events if corrupted.","sacrifices":"Two sources of data (Event Store is authoritative, projection is derived) introduces potential consistency lag. Developers must maintain the projection logic. Slightly more code than pure ES where current state is always derived from replay.","alternatives_considered":["Pure event sourcing — all reads via event replay or projections. Rejected: disproportionate complexity for the operational read path, which is simple key-based lookups.","Database temporal tables (e.g., PostgreSQL temporal extensions, SQL Server system-versioned tables) — simpler but less flexible for field-level change queries and cross-aggregate correlation. Considered viable for teams with low ES experience; see escalation.","Separate audit trigger table — INSERT triggers capture changes. Fragile, bypassed by bulk updates, and tightly coupled to schema. Rejected."]},{"decision":"Append-only Event Store as a dedicated persistence layer rather than reusing the existing RDBMS with soft deletes","gains":"Physical immutability guarantee (no UPDATE/DELETE permissions). Clear separation of concerns. Can use purpose-built event store (EventStoreDB) or a simple append-only table with appropriate DB permissions.","sacrifices":"Additional infrastructure component to operate. Migration complexity from current system.","alternatives_considered":["Append-only table in existing RDBMS with revoked UPDATE/DELETE permissions — viable and simpler operationally. Recommended as the starting point before considering a dedicated event store product."]},{"decision":"Before/after values in event payloads rather than computing diffs on read","gains":"O(1) field-change queries without replay. Compliance team can query for 'all changes to income field' directly. Simpler audit read model.","sacrifices":"Larger event payloads. Denormalization of data. Event producer must capture prior state before applying the change.","alternatives_considered":["Store only the new state in events and compute diffs by comparing consecutive events. Saves storage but makes field-level queries expensive."]},{"decision":"Snapshotting every N events rather than relying solely on replay","gains":"Bounded reconstruction time regardless of aggregate age. Loan applications can have 100s of events over months.","sacrifices":"Snapshot maintenance logic. Snapshots must be versioned alongside event schema.","alternatives_considered":["No snapshots — replay from beginning every time. Acceptable for aggregates with <50 events but degrades for long-lived loans."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Event schema evolution","evolution_path":"Events include a schemaVersion field. Upcasters transform old event versions to new versions on read. This allows the event schema to evolve without migrating historical events — critical for a long-lived immutable log."},{"area":"Read model proliferation","evolution_path":"New compliance requirements can be met by adding new projections that consume existing events. No changes to the write path required. Example: a new regulatory report requires grouping changes by officer — add a new projection, replay history, done."},{"area":"Migration to full event sourcing","evolution_path":"The hybrid approach leaves a clean path to full ES. If domain complexity grows (e.g., complex workflow orchestration, saga patterns), the current-state projection can be replaced with event-replay-based reads. The Event Store is already authoritative."},{"area":"Event Store technology","evolution_path":"Start with an append-only PostgreSQL table. If throughput or query requirements grow, migrate to EventStoreDB or Kafka with compaction disabled. The interface (append, read-forward) is the same."},{"area":"Audit query capabilities","evolution_path":"Start with SQL queries against the Event Store table. As query complexity grows, add Elasticsearch or a columnar store as a dedicated audit read model. Events are replayed to populate it."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Event immutability","reason":"Once written, events must never be modified or deleted. This is a regulatory requirement and the foundation of the entire architecture. Any correction must be a compensating event. This constraint must be enforced at the infrastructure level (DB permissions, not just application logic)."},{"area":"Event schema backward compatibility","reason":"Because events are immutable and retained indefinitely, every schema change must be backward compatible or handled by upcasters. Breaking changes to event schemas would require migrating the entire event log — extremely costly and risky."},{"area":"Aggregate consistency boundary","reason":"One loan application = one aggregate. All changes to a loan application must go through the aggregate root to ensure invariants are protected and events are consistently emitted. No direct table updates allowed."},{"area":"Actor identification on every event","reason":"Regulatory requirement: every change must record who made it. The system must reject any mutation that doesn't include a valid actor identity. No anonymous writes."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Event schema evolution becomes painful as the system ages. Over years, dozens of event versions accumulate, and upcaster chains become fragile.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Establish a strict event schema governance process from day one. Use a schema registry. Prefer adding optional fields over changing existing ones. Test upcaster chains in CI. Consider periodic 'event log compaction' where historical events are transformed to the latest schema in a controlled migration (with full audit of the migration itself)."},{"risk":"Projection lag causes operational users to see stale data (e.g., loan officer updates status but dashboard still shows old status).","severity":"medium","mitigation":"For the write path, return the new state directly from the command response (write-through). Only the broader read model is eventually consistent. For critical UIs, implement read-your-own-writes by including the expected event version in read requests."},{"risk":"Event Store becomes a single point of failure — if unavailable, no mutations can be recorded.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Treat the Event Store with the same HA/DR rigor as the primary database. If using PostgreSQL, standard replication and backup strategies apply. Consider synchronous replication for the event log. Implement circuit breakers and queuing for transient failures."},{"risk":"Migration from current update-in-place system to event-sourced system is complex and risky. Historical data has no events.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Phase the migration: (1) Introduce the Event Store alongside the existing table — dual-write during transition. (2) Generate synthetic 'LoanApplicationImported' events for all existing records to establish a baseline. (3) Gradually route reads to projections. (4) Decommission direct table writes. Run both systems in parallel with reconciliation checks."},{"risk":"Developers bypass the Command Service and write directly to the loan_applications table, creating unaudited changes.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Revoke INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE permissions on the loan_applications table for application database users. Only the projection service account can write to it. Enforce at the infrastructure level, not just code review."},{"risk":"Compliance queries over large event volumes become slow without proper indexing and read model optimization.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Design audit read model indexes from day one based on known compliance query patterns (by actor, by time range, by field, by aggregate). Implement pagination. Consider a dedicated analytical store for complex cross-aggregate queries."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (4-6 weeks): Add an append-only events table to the existing PostgreSQL database. Schema: (event_id UUID PK, aggregate_id UUID, aggregate_type, event_type, version INT, payload JSONB, actor_id, actor_type, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ, causation_id, correlation_id, schema_version INT). Add a UNIQUE constraint on (aggregate_id, version) for optimistic concurrency. REVOKE UPDATE, DELETE on this table for all application roles. Modify the existing application to emit events on every loan application mutation. The existing loan_applications table becomes a projection updated by event consumers. Phase 2 (2-3 weeks): Build the Point-in-Time Reconstruction Service and Audit Query API. Phase 3 (2-3 weeks): Add snapshotting, build compliance dashboards, and migrate historical data via synthetic import events.","files_to_create_or_modify":["db/migrations/001_create_loan_events_table.sql — append-only events table with proper constraints and indexes","db/migrations/002_revoke_direct_write_permissions.sql — lock down loan_applications table to projection service only","src/loan/domain/events.ts (or equivalent) — domain event types: LoanApplicationCreated, LoanApplicationStatusChanged, LoanApplicationFieldUpdated, LoanApplicationImported, etc.","src/loan/domain/loan-application.aggregate.ts — aggregate root that validates commands and emits events","src/loan/infrastructure/event-store.repository.ts — append and read operations against the events table","src/loan/infrastructure/loan-application.projection.ts — event consumer that maintains current-state table","src/loan/application/command-handlers/ — handlers for each command (UpdateStatus, ModifyField, AssignOfficer, etc.)","src/audit/reconstruction.service.ts — point-in-time state reconstruction from events","src/audit/query.api.ts — compliance query endpoints","src/loan/infrastructure/snapshot.repository.ts — snapshot persistence and retrieval"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use explicit command objects for all mutations. Every command handler follows: load aggregate (from projection or replay) → validate → emit events → persist events → return. Events are the source of truth; projections are disposable. Use a correlation ID that flows from the initial API request through all resulting events to enable end-to-end tracing. Apply the Outbox Pattern if events also need to be published to external systems (message bus) to avoid dual-write problems.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT store derived or computed data in events — events should capture raw facts (what changed) not conclusions (what the change means). Derived data belongs in projections.","Do NOT allow any code path to mutate loan application state without going through the aggregate and emitting an event. One unemitted change breaks the entire audit trail.","Do NOT use database triggers for event emission — triggers are invisible, hard to test, and bypass domain logic.","Do NOT embed the full loan application state in every event — only capture the changed fields with before/after values. Full-state events bloat storage and make field-level queries harder.","Do NOT forget to handle event schema versioning from day one. The first event you write will exist forever. Plan for it.","Do NOT build a custom event store framework when a simple append-only table with a version-based concurrency check will suffice. Avoid over-engineering."]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_constraints","description":"Database technology and infrastructure constraints are unknown. The recommendation assumes PostgreSQL with JSONB support, but the actual database, hosting environment (cloud provider, managed vs self-hosted), and operational maturity (does the team have experience running event stores?) are not specified. If the team is on a managed cloud database (e.g., AWS Aurora, Azure SQL), the approach to revoke permissions and enforce append-only semantics differs. If they're considering a dedicated event store product (EventStoreDB, Axon), the implementation guidance changes substantially.","options":["PostgreSQL append-only table (recommended for most teams — leverages existing infrastructure)","EventStoreDB (purpose-built, excellent for teams committed to ES, but new operational burden)","Kafka with infinite retention (overkill for this use case unless they already have Kafka infrastructure)","Cloud-native options: AWS DynamoDB streams, Azure Cosmos DB change feed (if already on these platforms)"],"recommendation":"Start with PostgreSQL append-only table unless the team already operates EventStoreDB or has compelling reasons to introduce new infrastructure."},{"type":"architectural_ambiguity","description":"The degree of event sourcing is a spectrum. The compliance requirements can be met by three distinct architectural approaches with materially different complexity profiles. (A) Full event sourcing where events are THE persistence model. (B) Hybrid: current-state table + append-only event log (recommended). (C) Database temporal tables + audit columns. Approach C is dramatically simpler but less flexible for cross-aggregate audit queries and future requirements. If the compliance requirements are strictly limited to what's stated (history, reconstruction, immutability) and won't expand, Approach C may be sufficient.","options":["A: Full event sourcing — maximum flexibility, maximum complexity. Choose if domain workflows are complex (sagas, compensating transactions) or if the team has ES experience.","B: Hybrid event sourcing — strong balance. Choose if audit is the primary driver but operational simplicity matters.","C: Temporal tables — minimum complexity. Choose if the team is small, requirements are stable, and cross-aggregate audit queries are not needed."],"recommendation":"Approach B (hybrid) — delivers full compliance with manageable complexity and a clean upgrade path to A if needed. Would need to validate with the team that temporal tables (Approach C) are insufficient before committing to the higher complexity of B."}],"confidence":{"level":58,"high_confidence_areas":["The compliance requirements are a textbook fit for event-sourcing patterns — the architectural direction is sound","Immutable append-only event log is the correct foundation regardless of which approach is chosen","CQRS separation of read and write models is appropriate given the divergent access patterns (operational reads vs audit queries)","Snapshotting will be necessary for long-lived loan application aggregates","Event schema versioning must be addressed from day one"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the team's operational maturity and experience level supports event sourcing vs simpler temporal tables","The actual database technology and infrastructure constraints","Whether compliance requirements will expand (favoring full ES) or remain stable (favoring temporal tables)","Performance characteristics of the existing system and acceptable latency for audit queries","Volume of loan applications and events — affects storage and query strategy"],"assumptions":["PostgreSQL or similar RDBMS is the current database","The engineering team has moderate experience but has not operated an event-sourced system before","Loan applications have a lifecycle of weeks to months with 50-200 state changes each","The compliance requirements will expand over time as regulations evolve","The system is not yet at a scale where a dedicated event store product is justified"]}}

The compliance requirements (full field-change history, point-in-time reconstruction, immutable audit log) are non-negotiable and perfectly aligned with event sourcing's strengths.
